jokes.text: We were fighting over which one of us loves you more.
jokes.setup: Homer asks what the problem is with the kids fighting
jokes.punchline: Instead of typical sibling rivalry, they're fighting over who loves their father more
jokes.explanation: Subverts typical sibling fighting with wholesome twist, establishes family dynamics early

jokes.text: You love him more. / No, you do. / No, I don't. / Yes, you do!
jokes.setup: Kids claim to love Homer more
jokes.punchline: They immediately start arguing in reverse - trying to give away the 'burden' of loving him most
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal - from loving competition to trying to avoid responsibility. Great economy and structure.

jokes.text: I'm trying to get the unfortunate noises out of my system while I can.
jokes.setup: Marge complains about Homer's eating behavior
jokes.punchline: Homer's pragmatic explanation for his crude behavior as pre-emptive embarrassment control
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - attempting responsibility through irresponsibility. Good character integration.

jokes.text: That's the only time he's ever spoken to me without using the word 'bonehead.'
jokes.setup: Homer mentions Mr. Burns liked Marge's gelatin once
jokes.punchline: Reveals the boss normally calls him 'bonehead' in every interaction
jokes.explanation: Establishes Homer's workplace status and Burns' character efficiently. Good economy.

jokes.text: Tough choice. / I'm picking respect.
jokes.setup: Marge asks kids to show Homer love and/or respect
jokes.punchline: Kids treat it like a difficult choice, with Lisa strategically picking respect over love
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy - treats basic parental expectation as strategic decision. Lisa's pragmatism shines.

jokes.text: Not me. I had to miss little league for this. / Quiet, Tom.
jokes.setup: Burns welcomes families to picnic
jokes.punchline: Random kid complains about missing baseball, gets shut down by parent
jokes.explanation: Basic background comedy showing other families' dynamics. Functional but unremarkable.

jokes.text: Fire that man, Smithers. I don't want him or his unpleasant family to ruin my picnic. / He'll be gone by the tug-of-war, sir.
jokes.setup: Burns hosts company picnic welcoming families
jokes.punchline: Immediately plots to fire Homer and family, with Smithers providing specific timeline
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns characterization - superficial hospitality masking cruelty. Smithers' efficiency adds to the joke.

jokes.text: And this must be, uh, Brat. / Bart. / Don't correct the man, Brat.
jokes.setup: Burns tries to remember the kids' names
jokes.punchline: Gets Bart's name wrong, Homer tells him not to correct the boss while using the wrong name
jokes.explanation: Excellent layered joke - Burns' mistake, Homer's misguided correction. Perfect character work and quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, for the love of Peter. That's all anybody brought. Some damn fool went around telling everyone I love that slimy goop.
jokes.setup: Homer proudly presents gelatin desserts Burns supposedly loves
jokes.punchline: Burns reveals he hates gelatin, someone spread false information
jokes.explanation: Good callback payoff revealing Homer misunderstood. Burns' colorful language adds character.

jokes.text: Hear that, Dad? You can lie around in your underwear and scratch yourself.
jokes.setup: Burns tells them to make themselves at home
jokes.punchline: Bart translates this into Homer's actual home behavior
jokes.explanation: Bart's literal interpretation exposes Homer's crude habits. Good character insight and timing.

jokes.text: No, just congratulating the son on a fine joke about his old man.
jokes.setup: Burns asks if there's trouble after Homer grabs Bart
jokes.punchline: Homer awkwardly covers by pretending Bart's insult was a compliment
jokes.explanation: Homer's desperate attempt to save face. Solid cringe comedy but not exceptional.

jokes.text: D-oh! Be normal. Be normal!
jokes.setup: Homer tells family to act normal, Bart immediately jumps in fountain
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation followed by desperate mantra
jokes.explanation: Iconic 'D-oh!' moment with perfect follow-up. Establishes Homer's catchphrase in context.

jokes.text: Hey, isn't that your boy there torturing the swans? - Bart!
jokes.setup: Homer is trying to maintain the family's good image
jokes.punchline: Bart is immediately spotted doing something horrible to swans
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - immediately after Homer's speech about being normal, Bart does something distinctly abnormal

jokes.text: Gee, do you think we should leave the kids unsupervised? / You're right. There. [points at kids]
jokes.setup: Marge worries about leaving kids alone
jokes.punchline: Homer 'solves' the problem by just pointing at them from across the yard
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic from Homer. Perfect economy and structure - pointing as 'supervision.'

jokes.text: You remember the rules? / Shut my mouth, and let your boss win.
jokes.setup: Homer asks Bart about sack race rules
jokes.punchline: Rules are about corporate politics, not the actual race
jokes.explanation: Good satire of workplace dynamics. Homer teaching his son corporate survival.

jokes.text: I don't know who to love more: my son, Joshua, who's captain of the football team, or my daughter, Amber, who got the lead in the school play. Usually, I use their grades as a tiebreaker, but they both got straight A's this term. What's a mother to do?
jokes.setup: Other families at the picnic socializing
jokes.punchline: Mother's 'problem' is having too many successful children to choose from
jokes.explanation: Contrasts with Simpson family dysfunction but feels a bit overwritten and obvious.

jokes.text: Well, I sense greatness in my family. / Your family? / Well, it's a greatness that others can't see, but it's there.
jokes.setup: Other mother brags about perfect children
jokes.punchline: Marge claims invisible greatness in her obviously dysfunctional family
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Marge - delusional optimism about her family. Self-aware delusion works well.

jokes.text: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I think there's a little 'al-key-hol' in this punch.
jokes.setup: Marge drinking punch at the picnic
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'alcohol' in naive, childlike way
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge characterization - innocent mispronunciation reveals her sheltered nature.

jokes.text: Man, this is pathetic. I'm goin' for it!
jokes.setup: Sack race where Homer is supposed to let Burns win
jokes.punchline: Bart decides to abandon the plan and actually compete
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bart moment - rebellious spirit overcoming strategic thinking. Sets up physical comedy.

jokes.text: Close one this year.
jokes.setup: Burns obviously wins rigged sack race by huge margin
jokes.punchline: Describes crushing victory as 'close'
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns deadpan. Perfect economy and character consistency.

jokes.text: Thank you all for coming. But now it's time to say good-bye. Please get off my property until next year. I suggest you don't dawdle. The hounds will be released in ten minutes.
jokes.setup: Burns giving closing remarks at company picnic
jokes.punchline: Polite thanks escalates to property eviction threat with attack dogs
jokes.explanation: Excellent Burns characterization - polite facade quickly dropping to reveal cruelty. Perfect escalation structure.

jokes.text: Marge, knock it off. - Well, I'm sorry. I'm sorry. I'm sorry.
jokes.setup: Burns is making his farewell toast
jokes.punchline: Drunk Marge is being disruptive and over-apologetic
jokes.explanation: Drunk Marge's excessive apologizing during a formal moment creates more attention than staying quiet would

jokes.text: Get that man's name. I predict big things for him down at the power plant.
jokes.setup: Burns sees fake family unity from Homer bribing Bart for a kiss
jokes.punchline: Burns completely misreads the situation as genuine, wants to promote Homer
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - audience knows it's fake, Burns doesn't. Shows Burns' fundamental disconnect.

jokes.text: Kiss you? But, Dad, I'm your kid. / Bart, please? Five bucks for a kiss.
jokes.setup: Homer desperately needs to look like good father to Burns
jokes.punchline: Has to literally pay his son for affection, Bart finds this weird
jokes.explanation: Good cringe comedy showing Homer's desperation and terrible parenting instincts.

jokes.text: I have never seen such an obvious attempt to curry my favor. / Fabulous observation, sir. Just fabulous.
jokes.setup: Burns recognizes Homer's blatant manipulation
jokes.punchline: Smithers immediately does the same thing Burns just criticized
jokes.explanation: Excellent layered irony - Burns criticizes obvious flattery, Smithers immediately provides obvious flattery. Perfect character work.

jokes.text: That cornball routine? 'I love you, Daddy.' Give me a break. / I pity you. / Why?
jokes.setup: Bart mocks the fake affection display
jokes.punchline: Lisa pities him for being unable to show genuine emotion
jokes.explanation: Good character distinction - Bart's cynicism vs Lisa's emotional intelligence.

jokes.text: Okay. Rub-a-dub-dub, thanks for the grub.
jokes.setup: Homer wants family to say proper grace
jokes.punchline: Bart offers irreverent nursery rhyme version
jokes.explanation: Typical Bart irreverence. Simple but efficient character moment.

jokes.text: Dear Lord, thank you for this microwave bounty, even though we don't deserve it. I mean, our kids are uncontrollable hellions. Did you see them at the picnic? Of course you did. You're everywhere. You're 'omnivorous.'
jokes.setup: Homer trying to give proper grace
jokes.punchline: Prayer becomes family roast session, ends with 'omnivorous' instead of 'omnipresent'
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer prayer that becomes self-sabotage. 'Omnivorous' malapropism is perfect character touch.

jokes.text: O Lord, why did you smite me with this family?
jokes.setup: Homer's prayer already roasting his family
jokes.punchline: Escalates to blaming God for giving him this family
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of the prayer joke. Homer's cluelessness about appropriate prayer content.

jokes.text: Maybe we should move to a larger community.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks they're the worst family in town
jokes.punchline: Solution is to move somewhere where they'd be more average by comparison
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer logic - don't improve, just find lower standards. Perfect character consistency.

jokes.text: Look at that, kids. No fighting. No yelling. No belching. / The dad has his shirt on.
jokes.setup: Homer observing 'normal' family through window
jokes.punchline: Lisa's addition shows how low Simpson family standards are
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing family dysfunction. Lisa's observation reveals how normal 'shirt-wearing' seems abnormal to them.

jokes.text: These people are obviously freaks. / Oh, you think so?
jokes.setup: Family watching normal people have polite conversation
jokes.punchline: Bart calls the normal people 'freaks'
jokes.explanation: Good ironic reversal - dysfunctional family sees functional family as abnormal.

jokes.text: What are they doing? - They're having a conversation. They actually enjoy talking to each other.
jokes.setup: The Simpsons spy on another family
jokes.punchline: Basic human interaction is foreign enough to require explanation
jokes.explanation: Sad comedy - reveals how little genuine communication exists in the Simpson household

jokes.text: Papa, I believe I heard some rustling in the bushes. / Hmm. I did too. Better get the gun.
jokes.setup: Simpson family spying on seemingly perfect neighbors
jokes.punchline: Perfect family immediately escalates to gun violence over bush rustling
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion - seemingly perfect family is actually more violent than Simpsons. Dark comedy twist.

jokes.text: Whoa, look at this place. What a dump! / It's worse than you think. I just trampled this poor sap's flower bed. / Homer, this is our house.
jokes.setup: Homer commenting on terrible house condition
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's looking at his own house and destroyed his own garden
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection joke with excellent structure. Homer's cluelessness reaches peak levels.

jokes.text: What's gotten into Bobo? / I got some wieners in my pocket.
jokes.setup: Police dog acting strange while tracking
jokes.punchline: Officer has hot dogs in pocket, ruining tracking
jokes.explanation: Standard incompetent cop humor. The casual admission of having pocket wieners while on duty is mildly absurd but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: You know, Moe, my mom once said something that really stuck with me. She said, 'Homer, you're a big disappointment.'
jokes.setup: Homer about to share meaningful maternal wisdom
jokes.punchline: Wisdom is just his mother calling him a disappointment
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of 'mother's wisdom' trope. Perfect Homer self-awareness without real insight.

jokes.text: And God bless her soul, she was really on to something.
jokes.setup: Homer's mom called him a disappointment
jokes.punchline: Homer agrees she was right about him being disappointing
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of previous joke. Homer's complete acceptance of his inadequacy.

jokes.text: Don't blame yourself, Homer. You got dealt a bad hand. You got crummy little kids that nobody can control.
jokes.setup: Moe tries to console Homer about his family problems
jokes.punchline: His 'comfort' is just agreeing that Homer's kids are terrible
jokes.explanation: Classic Moe tactlessness - his attempt at sympathy just reinforces Homer's problems

jokes.text: You can't talk that way about my kids! Or at least two of them. / Why, you got two I haven't met?
jokes.setup: Homer defending his children from Moe's insults
jokes.punchline: Admits he only likes two of his three kids, Moe assumes there are more kids
jokes.explanation: Good layered joke - Homer's partial defense becomes admission of favoritism, Moe's logical confusion.

jokes.text: Hi, friends. I'm Dr. Marvin Monroe. Does this scene look familiar? If so, I can help.
jokes.setup: Family fighting in background
jokes.punchline: TV therapist directly addressing audience as if they have same problems
jokes.explanation: Standard TV commercial parody. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: The answer to life's problems aren't at the bottom of a bottle. They're on TV!
jokes.setup: Homer reflecting on drinking after seeing therapy ad
jokes.punchline: Replaces one vice (drinking) with another (TV)
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic subverting self-help wisdom. Quotable and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: The fat guy on TV? / You're sending us to a doctor who advertises on pro wrestling? / Boxing, Lisa, boxing. There's a world of difference.
jokes.setup: Kids questioning therapy choice based on TV advertising
jokes.punchline: Homer insists there's a big difference between wrestling and boxing advertising
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing Homer's misguided confidence in his distinctions.

jokes.text: Why scrimp now on the off chance that they'll actually get in some place?
jokes.setup: Marge worried about spending kids' college fund
jokes.punchline: Homer doubts his children will get into college anyway
jokes.explanation: Dark but realistic Homer logic about his children's academic prospects.

jokes.text: I guess I'd have needed a partial scholarship.
jokes.setup: Family discovers college fund is only $88.50
jokes.punchline: Lisa's understated reaction to impossibly small college fund
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa understatement. Mature reaction to devastating financial reality.

jokes.text: I guess I'd have needed a partial scholarship.
jokes.setup: The family discovers their college fund is only $88.50
jokes.punchline: Lisa's deadpan acknowledgment of the financial reality
jokes.explanation: Lisa's mature acceptance of her family's financial limitations, understated and realistic

jokes.text: Homer, couldn't we pawn my engagement ring instead? / Now, I appreciate that, honey, but we need $150 here!
jokes.setup: Marge offering to sacrifice her engagement ring
jokes.punchline: Homer's response implies the ring is worth less than the TV
jokes.explanation: Dark marriage humor about the relative value Homer places on material possessions. His dismissive response to her sacrifice reveals priorities.

jokes.text: Now, I appreciate that, honey, but we need $150 here!
jokes.setup: Marge offers to pawn her engagement ring
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses it because it won't raise enough money compared to TV
jokes.explanation: Shows Homer's skewed priorities - TV more valuable than wedding ring.

jokes.text: Homer, you're driving a stake through the hearts of those who love you.
jokes.setup: Homer pawning TV and spending all family money
jokes.punchline: Marge's overly dramatic metaphor for selling the TV
jokes.explanation: Marge's melodramatic language about TV loss. Good character moment but not exceptional.

jokes.text: There go my young-girl dreams of Vassar.
jokes.setup: Family spending money on therapy instead of college fund
jokes.punchline: Eight-year-old Lisa mourns loss of specific elite college dreams
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lisa character moment - precocious and specific academic ambitions at young age.

jokes.text: We would if we had one. / Lisa!
jokes.setup: Dr. Monroe says they'd recognize him from TV
jokes.punchline: Lisa points out they don't have a TV anymore
jokes.explanation: Good callback to TV sale. Lisa's literal-mindedness creates awkward moment.

jokes.text: Now, Doctor, that's not true. / Ogre is such a strong word.
jokes.setup: Dr. Monroe says family sees Homer as an ogre
jokes.punchline: Homer objects only to the specific word choice, not the concept
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy - Homer's superficial objection while missing the point.

jokes.text: These mallet things are padded with foam rubber. What's the point? / They'd work much better without the padding, Doc.
jokes.setup: Therapy using padded mallets for safe aggression
jokes.punchline: Simpsons want to remove safety features to cause real damage
jokes.explanation: Perfect Simpson family logic - therapy tools not violent enough. Shows their fundamental dysfunction.

jokes.text: Just testing. - Why, you-- No, Homer, not yet.
jokes.setup: Dr. Monroe explains the shock therapy system
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately starts shocking people 'to test it'
jokes.explanation: Bart's mischievous nature immediately subverts the therapy's intended purpose

jokes.text: How could you shock your little sister? - My finger slipped. - So did mine.
jokes.setup: The shock therapy session devolves into chaos
jokes.punchline: Everyone starts 'accidentally' shocking each other
jokes.explanation: Good escalation as the therapy backfires spectacularly with everyone joining in the 'accidents'

jokes.text: Wait a minute, Doc, your TV commercial said, 'family bliss or double our money back.'
jokes.setup: Therapy session ended in disaster
jokes.punchline: Homer remembers the money-back guarantee from TV ad
jokes.explanation: Good callback to commercial setup. Homer's consumer awareness kicks in at perfect time.

jokes.text: Just go, and never tell anyone you were here!
jokes.setup: Dr. Monroe forced to pay double money back
jokes.punchline: Desperately wants to hide his professional failure
jokes.explanation: Standard professional embarrassment moment. Functional but predictable.

jokes.text: It's not the money, as much as the feeling that we earned it.
jokes.setup: Family gets $500 from failed therapy
jokes.punchline: Marge finds satisfaction in 'earning' money by being too dysfunctional for therapy
jokes.explanation: Excellent ironic twist - Marge finding pride in family dysfunction as achievement.

jokes.text: Twenty-one inch screen, realistic flesh tones... and a little cart so we can wheel it into the dining room on holidays.
jokes.setup: Homer planning to buy new TV with therapy money
jokes.punchline: Describes elaborate TV features with special holiday mobility
jokes.explanation: Good Homer materialism and specific TV obsession. The cart detail shows his elaborate planning.
